4cb82c5ba9dc53bebc51e1207d3c43c49454bd7a: {"cards":[{"q":"What is cellular respiration?","a":"Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form.","cite":"Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form."},{"q":"What are the main components of cellular respiration?","a":"Cellular respiration may be described as a set of metabolic reactions and processes that take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP, with the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor, and then release waste products."},{"q":"What is aerobic cellular respiration?","a":"If the electron acceptor is oxygen, the process is more specifically known as aerobic cellular respiration.","cite":"If the electron acceptor is oxygen, the process is more specifically known as aerobic cellular respiration."},{"q":"What is anaerobic cellular respiration?","a":"If the electron acceptor is a molecule other than oxygen, this is anaerobic cellular respiration.","cite":"If the electron acceptor is a molecule other than oxygen, this is anaerobic cellular respiration."},{"q":"How does anaerobic cellular respiration differ from fermentation?","a":"Anaerobic cellular respiration is not to be confused with fermentation, which is also an anaerobic process, but it is not respiration, as no external electron acceptor is involved.","cite":"Anaerobic cellular respiration is not to be confused with fermentation, which is also an anaerobic process, but it is not respiration, as no external electron acceptor is involved."},{"q":"What type of reactions are involved in cellular respiration?","a":"The reactions involved in respiration are catabolic reactions, which break large molecules into smaller ones, producing ATP.","cite":"The reactions involved in respiration are catabolic reactions, which break large molecules into smaller ones, producing ATP."},{"q":"What nutrients are commonly used in cellular respiration?","a":"Nutrients that are commonly used by animal and plant cells in respiration include sugar, amino acids and fatty acids, and the most common oxidizing agent is molecular (O2)."},{"q":"What is the maximum yield of ATP from one glucose molecule during cellular respiration?","a":"Biology textbooks often state that 38 ATP molecules can be made per oxidized glucose molecule during cellular respiration.","cite":"Biology textbooks often state that 38 ATP molecules can be made per oxidized glucose molecule during cellular respiration."},{"q":"Why is the maximum yield of ATP not reached?","a":"However, this maximum yield is never quite reached because of losses due to leaky membranes as well as the cost of moving pyruvate and ADP into the mitochondrial matrix, and current estimates range around 29 to 30 ATP per glucose.","cite":"However, this maximum yield is never quite reached because of losses due to leaky membranes as well as the cost of moving pyruvate and ADP into the mitochondrial matrix, and current estimates range around 29 to 30 ATP per glucose."},{"q":"How efficient is aerobic metabolism compared to anaerobic metabolism?","a":"Aerobic metabolism is up to 15 times more efficient than anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose).","cite":"Aerobic metabolism is up to 15 times more efficient than anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose)."},{"q":"Where do post-glycolytic reactions take place in eukaryotic cells?","a":"The post-glycolytic reactions take place in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells, and in the cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells.","cite":"The post-glycolytic reactions take place in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells, and in the cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells."},{"q":"What is glycolysis?","a":"Glycolysis is a metabolic pathway that takes place in the cytosol of cells in all living organisms.","cite":"Glycolysis is a metabolic pathway that takes place in the cytosol of cells in all living organisms."},{"q":"What does glycolysis convert glucose into?","a":"The process converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate (pyruvic acid), generating energy in the form of two net molecules of ATP.","cite":"The process converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate (pyruvic acid), generating energy in the form of two net molecules of ATP."},{"q":"How many ATP molecules are produced during glycolysis?","a":"Four molecules of ATP per glucose are actually produced, but two are consumed as part of the preparatory phase.","cite":"Four molecules of ATP per glucose are actually produced, but two are consumed as part of the preparatory phase."},{"q":"What is the reaction equation for glycolysis?","a":"Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H+ + 2 H2O + energy","cite":"Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H+ + 2 H2O + energy"},{"q":"What additional ATP is used in glycolysis?","a":"An additional ATP is used to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the help of phosphofructokinase.","cite":"An additional ATP is used to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the help of phosphofructokinase."},{"q":"What happens to pyruvate after (PDC)."},{"q":"Where is the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex located?","a":"The PDC contains multiple copies of three enzymes and is located in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells and in the cytosol of prokaryotes.","cite":"The PDC contains multiple copies of three enzymes and is located in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells and in the cytosol of prokaryotes."},{"q":"What is produced during the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA?","a":"In the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, one molecule of NADH and one molecule of CO2 is formed.","cite":"In the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, one molecule of NADH and one molecule of CO2 is formed."},{"q":"What is another name for the citric acid cycle?","a":"The citric acid cycle is also called the Krebs cycle or the tricarboxylic acid and 1 FADH2 as hydrogen (proton plus electron) carrying compounds and 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP."},{"q":"What is the total yield from one glucose molecule in the
